chunk_summary: Preterm infants require corrected age adjustments for feeding readiness and growth monitoring, emphasizing neurodevelopmental cues, gradual texture progression, and early referral for therapy in cases of persistent feeding difficulties.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of corrected age in assessing feeding readiness and growth in preterm infants.
Identify neurodevelopmental cues for complementary feeding initiation in preterm infants.
Recognize early referral triggers for feeding therapy in preterm infants with persistent difficulties.
Learn strategies to prevent feeding aversion and caregiver anxiety during texture progression.
key_facts: Corrected age must be used for growth plotting and feeding readiness assessment in preterm infants.
Developmental cues for complementary feeding include good head control, ability to sit with support, and diminished tongue-thrust reflex.
Delays in introducing lumpy textures can reinforce feeding aversion; advancing too fast increases gagging risk.
Micronutrient prophylaxis (iron and vitamin D) should follow local policy for preterm infants.
Early referral is recommended for persistent choking, prolonged feeds, or caregiver exhaustion due to higher aspiration and growth failure risks.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Use corrected age when plotting weight and length/height and when judging readiness for textures and meal frequency. (WHO child growth standards)
document_key_topics: Corrected age feeding readiness in preterm infants
Early referral triggers for feeding therapy
